channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: How the Iran War Could Consolidate China’s Energy Dominance
item.description: Amid global oil and gas disruptions, China stands prepared for the electrostate era.

item.title: Trump Demands Iran’s ‘Unconditional Surrender’
item.description: The White House wants to help choose Tehran’s new leadership—and won’t settle for anything less.

item.title: The Clash of Hypocrisies
item.description: As Washington abandons any pretense of a just global order, citizens around the world will continue to demand one.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of Feb. 28: The U.S. and Israel strike Iran, Canada and India strengthen ties, and the U.S. launches joint military operations in Latin America.

item.title: Ukraine and the Paradox of National Conservatism
item.description: Why have defenders of national identity and sovereignty not embraced Kyiv’s cause?

item.title: Nigel Farage Isn’t So Unlikely
item.description: Retracing the road to Brexit, a new book reveals the deep roots of British Euroskepticism.

item.title: The US Strikes on Iran Are a Reminder to China: Power Is Power
item.description: Beijing has built a Middle East strategy centered on influence without military presence. The sudden Israeli-U.S. strikes on Iran proved the hard limited of that approach.

item.title: Amid War, Hezbollah Suffers a Political Defeat
item.description: This week, Lebanon’s longest-serving politician started hedging on Hezbollah.

item.title: Disapproval for Iran War Among Experts Is Sky-High
item.description: A poll of IR scholars reveals strong opposition to the conflict and fears about its risks.

item.title: Shadow Boxing With the Kremlin
item.description: A CIA veteran depicts Moscow’s espionage arsenal. What is the West going to do about it?

item.title: The Winners and Losers of the Iran War, One Week in
item.description: Closing the Strait of Hormuz is a nightmare for global energy markets.

item.title: No, Israel Did Not Compel the U.S. to Wage War on Iran
item.description: It’s a convenient argument that echoes an antisemitic trope.

item.title: China Really Thinks It Is Just Defending Itself
item.description: That’s what makes it so dangerous.

item.title: South Korea and the Philippines Expand Ties in Nuclear Energy and Critical Minerals
item.description: By expanding its footprint in Southeast Asia, South Korea seeks to share its expertise and deepen collaboration in key sectors.

item.title: Iran’s Wartime Leader Isn’t a Dealmaker
item.description: Ali Larijani has a pragmatic temperament—but a worldview shaped by the system he now leads.

item.title: Kevin Warsh Is in for a Rude Awakening
item.description: For years, Kevin Warsh, Donald Trump’s nominee to serve as the next chair of the US Federal Reserve, has been staking out policy positions that would almost certainly backfire if put into practice. Fortunately, market conditions and the rest of the central bank's board will still have a say in monetary policymaking.

item.title: Why China Is Closely Watching US Congressional Support for the Iran War
item.description: Republican support for the war may not be as solid as it appears – and that could have implications for a U.S. intervention in Taiwan.

item.title: China Won’t Play Security Patron for Iran
item.description: Beijing doesn’t want to imitate the United States’ alliance obligations.

item.title: What Turkey Wants in Iran
item.description: While avoiding protracted instability in Iran is vital to Turkey’s interests, so is ensuring that the Islamic Republic does not emerge victorious from the current war. Turkey's ideal scenario – a managed degradation of Iran’s ambitions and capabilities – might be best served by a Venezuela-style leadership transition.

item.title: ‘Like a Startled Crow’: The Precarious Life of Women Amid Myanmar’s Polycrisis
item.description: For many women in Myanmar, conjoined economic and security crises mean never being able to let their guard down and never being able to think more than “one day at a time.”

item.title: Coupang’s Data Breach and the Urgency of Data Governance Reform in South Korea
item.description: Why did the Coupang case escalate from a domestic regulatory matter into an international trade issue?

item.title: A Stronger Work Ethic Won’t Fix Advanced Economies
item.description: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz learned the wrong lesson on his recent trip to China. Advanced economies expand and remain competitive not through additional labor inputs but through capital deepening, technological progress, and total factor productivity growth.

item.title: Hong Kong Is Beijing’s New ‘Vanguard’ in the Contest for Financial Sovereignty
item.description: Beijing is formally repositioning Hong Kong from a neutral intermediary between Chinese and global capital into a “vanguard” of the state’s financial security architecture.

item.title: Lee Jae-myung Is a New Kind of President – and South Koreans Approve
item.description: Lee has redefined the South Korean executive by trading imperial secrecy for radical transparency and focusing on tangible results.

item.title: India and Canada Reset: Pragmatism Takes Precedence Over Recriminations
item.description: Carney has opted for energetic, pragmatic action – including repairing ties with India – in the face of U.S. instability.

item.title: Counterterrorism Expert Ajmal Sohail on Pakistan’s ISI Targeting the Chinese in Afghanistan
item.description: “By undermining Chinese projects [in Afghanistan], the ISI seeks to remind Beijing that Pakistan remains a critical gatekeeper for regional connectivity, especially through CPEC.”

item.title: Chile Bids Goodbye to Boric
item.description: The outgoing president created a new blueprint for the Latin American left.

item.title: Israel After the Iran War
item.description: The false promise of total victory.

item.title: Financial Insecurity, Not Immigration, Is Driving Populist Politics
item.description: Nearly a decade after Brexit and Donald Trump’s first election victory, populism is still often portrayed as a revolt by working-class voters struggling to keep up with economic change. But today’s electoral shifts reflect everyday forms of insecurity affecting a much broader segment of the population.

item.title: Friedrich Merz Gets Real
item.description: Germany’s chancellor is dragging his country into a new era. Will Germans go along?

item.title: As Tehran Burns, South Asia Trembles
item.description: The U.S.-Israel war on Iran is testing South Asia’s energy security, remittance economies, and sectarian equilibrium — all at once.

item.title: Reclaiming Democracy From the Market
item.description: Meritocracy promised a fairer society, but instead it has deepened the divide between winners and losers and eroded the dignity of work. As AI reorders economies and concentrates power in fewer and fewer hands, the question is no longer whether democracy needs saving, but whether we can save it in time.

item.title: Iran’s Energy-Warfare Strategy
item.description: In its response to Operation Epic Fury, Iran appears to be exploiting the structural fragility of the global energy trade, since even a temporary closure of the Strait of Hormuz could have far-reaching global consequences. If energy infrastructure continues to be targeted as well, the risk will extend beyond prices.

item.title: ‘Kyi Pyar-Gate’ Rocks Myanmar’s National Unity Government
item.description: A high-profile corruption case in the country's umbrella resistance organization has drawn backlash – and claims of divisive politics.

item.title: Will China Overplay Its Hand?
item.description: How Beijing’s confidence will shake up the Trump-Xi summit.

item.title: The Mirage of a New Middle East
item.description: War with Iran won’t reshape the region the way America wants.

item.title: Philippines Arrests 3 Defense Personnel Over China Spy Claims
item.description: The three arrested individuals reportedly relayed sensitive information to Beijing, including details of Philippine resupply missions in the South China Sea.

item.title: In Hindsight: Madame Nhu and the American War in Vietnam
item.description: Tran Le Xuan, South Vietnam's "first lady," was passionate in her support of the southern republic – and a fierce critic of American involvement in Indochina.

item.title: Thai Election Commission Certifies Remaining Parliamentary Seats
item.description: With 499 of 500 seats now certified, the newly elected parliament will convene to select a new prime minister within 15 days.
